speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Ralene Brasg. I'm conducting an interview with Bluma Doman. Today is the 18th of February, 1996. The interview is being conducted in Johannesburg, South Africa in English, tape five. You mentioned that you arrived at another camp. What was the name of this camp and where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Weisswasser. Weisswasser. Over there, it was a-- really a dead camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Weisswasser?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not far from Mittelsteine.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was Czechoslovakia. But Hitler had every-- everyone, all the counties. We arrived there. There was really a dead camp. In-- in front of our room, where we slept, there was-- there was a big military depot. They had ammunition over there and that-- next to our camp. And this was ammunition factory where we hardly got to work. You hardly got to eat. There were people over there, French, all nationalities, and Jewish girls-- all nationalities. And they told-- told us that we've been to Auschwitz. And they said, you don't believe what was going on there. We can tell you. My father went there, my mother went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally we start realizing that-- the seriousness of the whole situation. We've been in Weisswasser not too long. You hardly worked. You hardly got to eat. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know exactly how long we've been there. Maybe a few weeks. One morning, they-- they're calling us out to roll call, the Germans. Did your brother go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my brother? No. My brother was in Auschwitz. I was by myself. I didn't have nobody anymore. And those two friends were sent to another labor camp. I didn't have any more friends. My friends, we were separated. And I wasn't-- I wasn't still too very stable about my sickness, my pneumonia, but this camp was awful. Luckily, we were not too long in this camp. One day they called us out for roll call, and the German announced that they're leaving the camp. Somebody else will take it over. They was afraid to say the Russians. And there is plenty of food in the kitchen. If you want to go in, you go and help yourself. And they dispersed. You could see it on the-- on the village, it was also a village, [INAUDIBLE], was a village you could see it, how panicky they are, the-- the Poles, the Germans. They started running out of the village and a lot of us followed them, but we didn't do anything. But the villages, there lived a lot of Germans. They knew that we're freed from the camps. I swear to you, when you came out to the village, they would stand big barrels of potatoes and peels cooked, big barrels of eggs, big barrels of fruit, because they knew that we are going to come out and look for food. That's what we did. First, we did. I needed some shoes. The German lady said, go ahead and choose what you want. So I took some shoes. We'd been there a few days. The Russians came in. We went-- there was a-- there was an order that state blow up the depot with the ammunition, and we all go with them. But [INAUDIBLE], they didn't have enough time, and they left us alive. We went to the village.. We got food. Then the Czechs said it's dangerous to stay here. They took us to a school in the [INAUDIBLE]. Another day there were Russian soldiers already. They came in, I tell you, an army, exhausted, sick. And they-- we, we, the haftlings, had to feed the Russian soldiers. We-- we went into the village. They were scared of us. They gave us whatever. They made ready for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- we've been free. As I saw the-- the Russian tore open the gate of the camp. I stayed at the window, and I fell unconscious. Because the Germans said, You can go home. You can go home to your family. And I said to myself, Who's my family? Where I go? I fainted. I just fell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was awake. We didn't have in that camp any beds anymore. We slept on the floor because it was-- the defiant was under their feet already. So we've been in Weisswasser in a school, the Czechs. They're such golden people, you should see it. They brought every morning big, big pails of milk, fresh rolls. Should see it, the Czechs and they fed us. We stayed in that school. There was a big kitchen. They brought us food, we cooked, we even fed the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one room in the whole school a few hundred, but in one little room, they were-- were-- we cooked, we all ate together. I'll tell you another thing, when we got into the German kitchens, they were-- had so many dishes when we cook something to eat, after we ate we broke the plates, just like they did to us. Then in [INAUDIBLE], there was the Germans had to-- the attics.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went-- we did just like the Germans did. We were looking for-- for-- for clothing. We went up to the attics. We found a lot of clothes we shared out between us. Then comes the bad part. Go home. Where? To whom shall we go? Did you believe that the war was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: --ended.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was May 7. The war was ended. We been free. The Germans ran. They left everything. We've got into the-- the village. We get what we could. Most of us were barefoot, didn't have any shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now comes the question, go home? The Czechs came into the school and they say if we want to go home, we [CZECH]. Over here is written [CZECH]. I always remembered the elevators are written [CZECH], yes? And [CZECH] in Czech is a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always-- whenever I go out to the doctor, and I go up, especially in the general when I go to the clinic, I always see [CZECH]. See, over here it's called the elevator. We call in America elevator. And here is a [CZECH], elevator.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- so the Czechs came in they said we should move on, we should go home. Me, as I was oldest, I remember my father said to all of us, who's going to survive this war comes back to Ozorkow and wait for each other. So me, I went home. Home. How did we get home? There were trains. A little bit they could go. The rails were busted from-- from the Germans. You know? We got the [INAUDIBLE] as I came over there I found a few people from our town. And everybody embraced and everybody was excited that we lived the war. We gone home. Have you gone home? We almost walked big, big groups. At the end, we've been in the train on the roof. And over there-- as I came down, we stopped in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped. I-- I stay there, and I-- one fellow from Ozorkow comes and brays, oh, you lived. Good, good. He said, in Lodz, my-- my sister-in-law, the head of place, come I'm taking you home with me. That's what he did. On the-- on the way, so I'm coming home to Ozorkow. There were 10 [? Juden. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how long did you stay in the Lodz ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years. From 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, when you were on your way back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, on the way back was Lodz already. There wasn't any ghetto. Was Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there a few days. This guy took me home. And they were cooking over there, [INAUDIBLE], and chicken soup, and they were eating. And they kept me over there. Then I said, I want to go home to Ozorkow. Remember what my father told us. So I go to Ozorkow. There was there my husband. Six people-- no, two couples, 10 people all together. My husband was already there. The Secretary of the Committee. And as I arrived, my husband was at leave. We knew each other before the war. My husband was just one year older than I. And he embraced me, took me like he found a new family. So we stayed five days yet. I-- I did, I remembered what my father said, so I did myself a big favor. I walked up to the place where we lived and there opens a big Polish lady. And our furniture-- I was so heartbroken-- this was the second floor-- I fell down completely the steps. For so-- it was very emotional all the time. For my family is nothing in the world can-- can do anything. For my grandchildren, I'll kill. I said once to [INAUDIBLE], Irene, for my grandchildren, I'll kill. And she looked at me. Just a matter of speech, but she knew that I mean business. We had a maid. She was a [INAUDIBLE] between the children. So I fell down the steps. I came back, limping. My husband start bandaging my foot. And I thought, well, we've been in Ozorkow five days. No-- nobody shows up from my family. Everybody's crying. So there were those 10 people. Six of us decide we go leave Ozorkow. We go into Germany and the American zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you start [? walking ?] again. But to go with the-- you know, to the borders to go back to-- you had to go-- you had to cross Czechoslovakia then to go into Germany. So six of us. One-- one of the fellows who lives now in Atlanta, Georgia, he went in somewhere, and he-- he found a pair of SS boots. And he put himself on the boots. And when we came to the borders, and the Germans-- the-- the Americans saw him, he said [NON-ENGLISH]-- I am Gestapo. He said, no, I just-- just picked up a pair and I put on. I'll never forget this. So who were these six people that were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they're all alive, thank god. Well, there was one couple, us, yes, as we go back, the five days, which I was in Poland. My husband said, you want to get married? I said, of course, there's nobody here. We start crying, where's this, where's that. We got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband? Charles. Aaron Charles. Aaron-- And Aaron is named after him. Bless him. So my husband said-- I said, oh, yeah, I'm tired. He said, look, are you coming with me-- I was married already-- or you staying here? We are not staying over here with the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ozorkow. [INAUDIBLE] married us. We didn't have a ring. We took some-- something. Got married, when we came then to Germany, we had to go over everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was June-- June '44. May, yes. Or end of May, June '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah, this was '45. Yes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you use for a wedding ring?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just found something from a cigar or something. You know. And when I came-- we came to Germany, I'm wearing that ring. And we went back to Germany. It took us a long time. And the way we stopped. And we-- it wasn't anymore that we've been so hungry because where we stopped, we could always get from the villages some food. Finally we arrived in Bamberg, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1945, the end of May or the beginning of June. Something like that. We got into-- into America. The Jewish soldiers were more than happy to see us. We came in. We went into the American headquarters. We told them that we are displaced persons. He said, you need a place where to live? He put us-- he went into the Germans. They live in like the, beautiful-- you should see their furniture. He went in there, five, six rooms. He said, you have [? off the ?] kitchen and one room. Over here, this couple will stay. Over here, this couple. They're Americans who took us in. We-- and we settled. We settled there nicely. My husband, we adored each other. We had just each other. Now we started crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is the family? My husband was from a large family of seven children, five sisters and two brothers. And he was crying all the time. So wherever he had something in Germany, this was the American zone. Germany [INAUDIBLE]. This is Bavaria. And [? whenever ?] we heard something, I'm going. One day he's coming home. He said, come, sit shiva. I said, why? Somebody I met from Ozorkow, they told him, your father died in [INAUDIBLE], where my brothers are. Then he comes home another day. I hear Arieh is in Italy. He said, I'm going to Italy. It was easy to go. I said, when? He said, I'm going to Italy. I'm going to bring him, that we are together. He went to Italy. As he comes to Italy, [INAUDIBLE] port, a harbor. And he meets over there a man from Ozorkow, and he said, I'm looking for Arieh Sztorch. He said, you see? This boat is there. Your-- your-- daughter-- no. Brother-in-law is on this boat. My-- my son was the first boat which the England let in straight to Israel, up to Cypress. There were a boat only of children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my husband comes back. He said, I'm sorry, he was on the boat. I couldn't chase the boat. And he's going to Israel. So I satisfied myself. Now I'm looking for my sister, and I'm looking for-- my father I knew he was already gone. My mother is gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sit shiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seven days--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just whatever my husband said. My husband was a religious man and he knew. He said one hour is enough. I said shiva. My brother is not here, he comes back. I opened the door when he knocked on the door. I looked at my husband, opened the door, and I looked behind him. Maybe he hides my little brother. He said, no, Arieh is going to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I'm looking for my sister. You've got to listen to that. We lived in a nice apartment, my husband and myself. The owner, you know, the Joint Distribution, they gave us clothing. They gave us every week food. Right away [INAUDIBLE] open a butcher shop, a kosher butcher shop. It was already paradise. Whenever we had to stay in line, we didn't stay in line, we go first. The Germans last, we are first. Was-- was already America. And I worked with my husband, was on a Sunday. There was, where we lived, there was a little river and there was a bridge. And as we come to the bridge, a guy calls out. He comes to touch us. And he said, you are Helen's sister. I said, how do you know? You're not the [? Ozorkowian. ?] He said, the way she described me, you are her sister. I have to prove to you something. He took out the picture of my sister and on the back was written her address where she is. Where was she? In Belgium. So either way, I wrote her a letter. There was no-- no mail regularly in Germany, but American soldiers mailed for us. The Americans were wonderful when we survived that war.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Bamberg. We had a nice little flat. We have enough food. We have enough clothes. I start sewing. You know, we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you husband do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was a textile loom-- a [GERMAN]. He was making the designs of clothing. That's textiles. He could take a part a piece of material, and he-- I brought the books, and I had to [? lay ?] the books. He'd draw this and he put it in a book. [GERMAN] they're called in Germany. So we are happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: My husband didn't bring my brother. My sister's in Belgium. I wrote her a letter. She said she's going to Israel, which she did. She went to Israel and then met with my brother, together. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And we are in Germany. We are trying to go to Israel on the illegal [INAUDIBLE]. You know? They didn't let in the-- the Jews. The English. Did you see Peter Lawford said whenever you shake a tree, you-- you shake a Jew out. So we've been in Germany. My husband said, it was 1949 already. We've been four years married. And all of a sudden, a blessed event. I'm pregnant with my son. 1949, in February, our son was born. Four and a half months later, we've been in America. What's your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Mosche. That's all I have. And I have a daughter. That's all I have. Three and a half years later already, in America, my daughter was born. So we had two beautiful children, and my husband had a good job because he had an uncle. It was so amazing how people could help themselves. My husband said that once he met with a cousin, a first cousin, and he was jumping on the train to go somewhere. He said to this cousin, give me the address of my uncle in America. My husband's mother had the oldest brother in America. And he gave my husband the address verbally and when we came to Bamberg, my husband wrote a letter to uncle. It was the correct address. We got right away letters from Munich. And communication wasn't good yet. And the uncle said, I want to come to see you. So he said, Uncle, don't come. You come when we will settle. In Germany, in Bamberg, every Shabbos, every Friday night, we went American chaplains. They took the movie house, and we had services. As we've been there, every Jew, every day, every soldier adopted some Jewish family. Said, give me the address, give me the name, I'll write home. They got to send parcels.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- [? I didn't know ?] I was lucky with my husband. We adopt, I have the pictures, you'll see it. We adopted, no, as we've been in shul, he said that he couldn't speak German really. You know Fisher toys? [? Willie ?] Fisher. [? Willie ?] said to-- to me, give me-- I want to come-- are you married? I said, yes, that's my husband, in broken German.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I want to come to you for the [? late ?] Friday night, for the lights. You know, for the candles. I said, with pleasure. One-- one Friday evening, we had lit the candle, I made a supper. [? Willie ?] shows up with another [? Willie, ?] a friend with the [INAUDIBLE] and the [INAUDIBLE] lady. When she saw this, she-- she got scared to death. So they came and every time [? Willie ?] adopted us, me and my husband. And they were recovering parcels from all over America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone-- then one day we were sitting in the park in Bamberg. We're sitting in the park, my husband, myself, a fellow comes down, an American soldier. He said, do you speak German? And I said, yes, we speak. He said, I want to adopt you. I want to send home and-- to-- to give you help. Because his name was [? Ralph, ?] a German too. So I'm telling you this guy, with every relative he had in America, everybody was loading us with parcels. That's the American way. They're wonderful people. America is a wonderful country. I'm just sorry because I had leave. Now we-- we are American ready. My husband got a nice job. We had a nice place we had to live. We had still the little boy. Did you have to get-- apply for any papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh sure. The Joint Distribution they made fiction contracts from anybody as long as we can get down to-- to America, see? So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did the Joint Committee help you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband said to them, I don't want any charity. Give me a job. Said, why you-- why you worried? Sit. They took us into a store. They clothed us from head to toe, even a house coat. The Joint is one of the beautiful Jewish organization. They-- in Bamberg, they announced who wants tallis, who wants [? tefillin? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: My husband, I still got him here. My son brought it. A tallis-- I made them a tallis [INAUDIBLE] from-- from velvet of black. We have it and the [? tefillin, ?] from-- they gave us. The Yiddish cart, you want to do whatever you want. You want [NON-ENGLISH]? We have [NON-ENGLISH] too. [NON-ENGLISH], you want everything. Start-- a lot of Juden start going back to-- to that old life, to the religious life, to eat kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are [INAUDIBLE] when we arrived in-- in New York, and we called our uncle, we couldn't call him right away because we couldn't speak English. So my husband-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my husband went down to the lobby in the hotel. He found a gentleman fellow, a rich fellow, and he called my uncle. And Uncle right away replied to our hotel room. And he said, I'm coming. He-- my husband said, we are going to Rochester, New York. Just for New York. And we come there and said OK. And we said a little bit. So we came into Rochester, New York. The Joint Distribution, there's a place where to live.
